posts.title: Why Every Engineer Should Understand the Network Stack
posts.summary: Most developers treat networking as a black box. I spent years working on protocol layers at Alcatel-Lucent and it fundamentally changed how I debug, design, and reason about distributed systems.
posts.content:
When I started my career at Alcatel-Lucent, I was dropped into the deep end of the network stack. We were writing drivers for WCDMA base stations — software that sat just above the hardware and had to be correct, because correctness meant a cell tower working or not working. Millions of phone calls. Real consequences.

That experience gave me something most software engineers never develop: intuition for what's actually happening on the wire.

The stack is not an abstraction you can ignore

Modern web development has done a beautiful job of abstracting away the network. You write fetch(), you get data back. You run a Kubernetes cluster, pods communicate. It all just works — until it doesn't.

When it breaks, the engineers who understand what's underneath fix it in 20 minutes. Everyone else spends days adding logs and restarting services.

What I learned from reading packet traces

Spending months staring at Wireshark captures taught me a few things that no book could:

  • TCP isn't magic. Retransmissions, window scaling, and congestion control are all observable and tunable. High latency often has a very specific cause visible in a trace.
  • DNS is the source of half your mysteries. Timeouts that look like service failures are often DNS cache misses or TTL issues.
  • Packet loss is not random. It has patterns — buffer overflows, bad NICs, misconfigured switches. If you can capture, you can find it.

The practical takeaway

You don't need to write kernel modules to benefit from this knowledge. Spend a few hours with Wireshark. Read the TCP/IP Illustrated series. Next time you have a mysterious network issue in production, you'll have a mental model that actually matches reality.

The engineers I most respect can navigate fluidly between the application layer and the transport layer. That fluency is rare and incredibly valuable.
posts.prompt: Why engineers should understand the network stack — based on my experience at Alcatel-Lucent

posts.title: What Software-Defined Networking Actually Changes
posts.summary: SDN was supposed to revolutionize networking. Having worked on VMware NSX, here's my honest take on what it delivers, what it doesn't, and where it actually matters.
posts.content:
Before VMware, I thought SDN was mostly marketing. Network virtualization sounds like something that belongs in a vendor slide deck, not in a production data center.

After two years working on NSX, I changed my mind — but not for the reasons the marketing says.

The real value isn't flexibility, it's programmability

Everyone sells SDN as 'flexible networking.' What that actually means in practice: you can write a Python script to create a network topology instead of SSHing into 40 switches. That difference matters at scale.

At VMware, the moment that clicked for me was watching a new tenant environment spin up in seconds — L2 network, firewall rules, load balancer config — all from an API call. The same process on physical hardware would have taken a human days and still would have had a config error somewhere.

Where SDN genuinely struggles

Overlay networks add latency. VXLAN encapsulation isn't free, and on high-throughput paths you feel it. For most workloads this is negligible. For latency-sensitive financial systems or real-time applications, it's a real tradeoff.

Also: debugging is harder. When something breaks in a physical network, you can walk up to a switch. In an overlay, you're staring at virtual tunnels and trying to figure out if the issue is in the overlay, the underlay, or the VM itself. Tooling has improved, but it's still harder.

The honest picture

SDN is genuinely transformative for large-scale cloud infrastructure. For a 50-server shop, physical networking is probably simpler. The technology is real, the tradeoffs are real, and the sweet spot is bigger than skeptics think but smaller than vendors claim.
posts.prompt: Honest take on SDN and what it actually changes, from working on VMware NSX
